items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: Why do Jews not accept Jesus as the Messiah?
items.correct: Because the Jewish Messiah is expected to bring peace, rebuild the Temple and restore Israel — events the Jewish tradition holds Jesus did not accomplish.
items.misconception: Because Jews believe Jesus never existed.
items.random_wrong: Because Jesus was crucified, and the Messiah cannot die.
items.why: Most Jewish thinkers accept Jesus existed as a historical figure. The disagreement is about whether he fulfilled messianic prophecies — a theological judgement, not denial of history.

items.correct: The Islamic declaration of faith: 'There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah' — affirming monotheism and Muhammad's prophethood.
items.misconception: The Shahada is the requirement to pray five times a day.
items.random_wrong: The Shahada is the Islamic pilgrimage to Mecca.
items.why: Students confuse the five pillars. Shahada is the declaration, Salah is prayer, Hajj is pilgrimage — each is a distinct pillar with a different function.

items.q: Which encyclical banned Catholic contraception in 1968?
items.why: Correct answer: Humanae Vitae. “Rerum Novarum” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Maximum wives allowed under Islamic law?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 wives. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
